summary: Drift survival-curve experiment run-1: ungated vs gated planning chains, Sonnet-locked
logs: Chains and extraction done; running seed positive control

additions.summary: Input validation with well-formed error envelope definitions and rejection of invalid inputs with a well-formed error response
Basic request routing to a stub processing layer in Phase 1
Input contract documented and verified to match implemented behaviour
Lifecycle state enumeration and state-transition rules for primary entities
Invariant enforcement at the persistence boundary (state transitions that violate invariants rejected with descriptive error)
Database migrations must be idempotent and apply cleanly against an empty database
Domain model documentation including entity relationships and lifecycle diagram
Compensating transactions or rollback paths required where a step fails mid-workflow, restoring consistent state
Test coverage of at least one test per acceptance-criteria condition in the processing/business-logic phase
Domain events emitted on state transitions with schema definitions and consumer stubs
Internal service-boundary interfaces enforced by types or contracts so callers cannot bypass them
Contract test for each integration-point adapter against a stub or sandbox
Load and error-rate testing with performance tuning if needed
External API documentation published as OpenAPI spec or equivalent, matching implemented behaviour
End-to-end tests pass against a production-equivalent environment
Deployment completes without manual intervention and feature is reachable in target environment
Runbook covers how to deploy, how to roll back, and how to diagnose the top three failure modes
Valid inputs are accepted and forwarded to a processing stub, and invalid inputs are rejected with a well-formed error response (Phase 1 acceptance criteria).
Input contract is documented and matches implemented behaviour (Phase 1 acceptance criteria).
Entities support full CRUD through repository interfaces (Phase 2 acceptance criteria).
State transitions that violate invariants are rejected with a descriptive error (Phase 2 acceptance criteria).
Schema migrations are idempotent and apply cleanly against an empty database (Phase 2 acceptance criteria).
Domain model is documented including entity relationships and a lifecycle diagram (Phase 2 acceptance criteria).
Rollback paths restore consistent state when a step fails mid-workflow (Phase 3 acceptance criteria).
Test coverage includes at least one test per acceptance-criteria condition in Phase 3 (Phase 3 acceptance criteria).
Domain events are emitted on state transitions with schema definitions and consumer stubs (Phase 4 scope and acceptance criteria).
Integration-point adapters each have a contract test against a stub or sandbox (Phase 4 acceptance criteria).
Internal interfaces are enforced by types or contracts so callers cannot bypass them (Phase 4 acceptance criteria).
Callers receive output in the documented shape for every workflow outcome (Phase 4 acceptance criteria).
Runbook covers at minimum: how to deploy, how to roll back, and how to diagnose the top three failure modes (Phase 5 acceptance criteria).
End-to-end tests pass against a production-equivalent environment (Phase 5 acceptance criteria).
Deployment completes without manual intervention and the feature is reachable in the target environment (Phase 5 acceptance criteria).
External API documentation (OpenAPI spec or equivalent) is published and matches implemented behaviour (Phase 5 acceptance criteria).
Input validation with well-formed error responses for invalid inputs
Input contract must be documented and match implemented behaviour
Lifecycle state enumeration and state-transition rules for the primary entity
State transitions that violate invariants are rejected with a descriptive error
Migrations are idempotent and apply cleanly against an empty database
Domain model must be documented including entity relationships and lifecycle diagram
Compensating transactions or rollback paths required where a step fails mid-workflow
Test coverage must include at least one test per acceptance-criteria condition in Phase 3
Domain events emitted on state transitions with schema definitions and consumer stubs
Internal interfaces enforced by types or contracts — callers cannot bypass them
Each integration-point adapter must have a contract test against a stub or sandbox
Runbook must cover: how to deploy, how to roll back, and how to diagnose the top three failure modes
End-to-end tests must pass against a production-equivalent environment
External API documentation published as OpenAPI spec or equivalent and must match implemented behaviour
Deployment completes without manual intervention and the feature is reachable in the target environment
Structured logging, metrics, and distributed tracing instrumentation required, with key workflow steps queryable in the observability stack
Logs for each workflow execution must include a correlation identifier and outcome (Phase 4 acceptance criterion specifies this as a concrete constraint on log structure).
A health check must pass when healthy and fail with a meaningful message when a dependency is unavailable (Phase 4 acceptance criterion defines this specific pass/fail behavior).
Performance and load validation against realistic data volumes is required before delivery (Phase 5 scope item — a concrete testing constraint not implied by any claim).
A smoke test covering the end-to-end critical path must execute successfully after a clean deployment (Phase 5 acceptance criterion — a specific operational gate).
Phase 1 requires all domain entities defined with fields and relationships, and a data access layer supporting create, read, update, and delete for each entity verified by automated tests.
Phase 1 mandates that no application or workflow logic is coupled to the storage implementation.
Phase 2 requires each key workflow to be executable through an automated integration test covering the happy path.
Phase 2 requires domain invariants to be enforced with violations returning well-typed errors.
Phase 2 requires workflows to be decoupled from the delivery mechanism (HTTP, CLI, etc.).
Phase 3 requires every external contract to have a schema definition checked into the repository.
Phase 3 requires boundary tests that confirm valid payloads are accepted and invalid payloads are rejected with descriptive errors.
Phase 3 requires that a contract-breaking change to an internal type does not silently pass contract tests.
Phase 4 requires structured logging at workflow entry/exit and error boundaries including a correlation identifier and outcome per execution.
Phase 4 requires a health check that passes when healthy and fails with a meaningful message when a dependency is unavailable.
Phase 4 requires a runbook covering at least the failure modes surfaced during Phase 2 and 3 testing.
Phase 5 requires performance and load validation against realistic data volumes.
Phase 5 requires all acceptance criteria from Phases 1-4 to pass in the target environment.
Phase 5 requires no known P0/P1 defects to remain open.
Phase 5 requires a smoke test covering the end-to-end critical path that executes successfully after a clean deployment.
Domain model phase requires no application or workflow logic to be coupled to the storage implementation (decoupling constraint).
Data access layer must support create, read, update, and delete for each entity, verified by automated tests.
Each key workflow must be executable through an automated integration test covering the happy path.
Domain invariants are enforced and violations must return well-typed errors.
Workflows must be decoupled from delivery mechanism (HTTP, CLI, etc.).
Every external contract must have a schema definition checked into the repository.
Boundary tests must confirm valid payloads are accepted and invalid payloads are rejected with descriptive errors.
A contract-breaking change to an internal type must not silently pass the contract tests.
Logs for each workflow execution must include a correlation identifier and outcome.
A health check must pass when the system is healthy and fail with a meaningful message when a dependency is unavailable.
A runbook must cover at least the failure modes surfaced during Phase 2 and Phase 3 testing.
Performance and load validation against realistic data volumes is required in Phase 5.
A smoke test covering the end-to-end critical path must execute successfully after a clean deployment.
No known P0/P1 defects may remain open at delivery.
A downstream reviewer must confirm whether the upstream brief was intentionally empty or erroneously omitted.
If the brief was erroneously omitted, a corrected brief must be supplied and the plan regenerated.
If the empty brief was intentional, the document is accepted as a valid no-op artifact and the chain proceeds to the next stage without further action.
A downstream reviewer must confirm whether the upstream brief was intentionally empty or erroneously omitted before the chain proceeds.
If the brief was erroneously omitted, a corrected brief must be supplied and the plan regenerated.
If the empty brief was intentional, this document is accepted as a valid no-op artifact and the chain proceeds to the next stage without further action.

The approach document must be present so that downstream implementation planners have a file to inspect, even when that file signals a no-op — establishing a structural contract that a placeholder artifact is always emitted rather than nothing.

The document must clearly state that no implementation work follows from it, so planners do not attempt to derive tasks from it — establishing an explicit prohibition on downstream task derivation from this artifact.

A reviewer confirmation step is the sole required action before the chain can advance — establishing a mandatory human gate that blocks progression without a reviewer sign-off on whether the empty brief was intentional or erroneous.

If the brief is found to be erroneous, the plan is regenerated and the approach stage reruns from scratch — establishing a defined remediation path (full restart of plan and approach stages) as a contractual process obligation.

A 5-step chain pass-through workflow is defined: plan-writer receives empty brief and emits no-op plan; approach-writer receives no-op plan and emits no-op approach; a downstream reviewer confirms whether the empty brief was intentional or erroneous; if intentional the chain proceeds treating the approach as a valid placeholder; if erroneous a corrected brief is supplied, the plan is regenerated, and the approach stage reruns from scratch.

The approach document must be present so downstream implementation planners have a file to inspect even when it signals a no-op — establishing a structural delivery contract that a placeholder document is always emitted.

The document must clearly state that no implementation work follows from it, so planners do not attempt to derive tasks from it — establishing an explicit no-op signaling obligation on the artifact itself.
The reviewer confirmation step (acceptance criterion 1 from the business plan) is identified as the sole required action before the chain can advance.

The approach introduces a five-step pass-through chain: (1) plan-writer receives empty brief and emits a no-op plan, (2) approach-writer emits a no-op approach, (3) a downstream reviewer confirms whether the empty brief was intentional or erroneous, (4) if intentional the chain proceeds treating the approach as a valid placeholder, (5) if erroneous a corrected brief is supplied and the plan and approach are regenerated from scratch.
A contractual obligation is stated that the approach document must be present so downstream implementation planners have a file to inspect, even when that file signals a no-op.
A contractual obligation is stated that the document must clearly state that no implementation work follows from it, so planners do not attempt to derive tasks from it.
The reviewer confirmation step (acceptance criterion 1 from the business plan) is identified as the sole required action before the chain can advance.
A reviewer role examines the technical approach document and records a written decision (intentional or erroneous) with a dated sign-off artifact (comment, ticket update, or explicit sign-off).
If the decision is erroneous, a corrected brief must be attached or linked to the decision artifact before the chain can proceed.
On the intentional path, downstream implementation planners must be notified that no tasks are to be derived from the no-op approach.
On the erroneous path, the chain is halted and plan regeneration is explicitly queued.
Exactly one path (intentional or erroneous) is active simultaneously — both paths cannot be active at once.
On the rerun path, the corrected brief is fed sequentially to the plan-writer stage first, then the resulting plan to the approach-writer stage, producing a replacement approach document.
On the rerun path, the original no-op plan and approach must be archived or clearly marked as superseded.
Chain metadata is updated with a status value of either 'no-op confirmed' or 'rerun completed' (not left ambiguous), a timestamp, and a reference to the Phase 1 decision trail.
Downstream consumers must be able to determine, without reading the full chain, whether any tasks follow from the run.

A reviewer must examine the technical approach document and record a decision artifact (comment, ticket update, or explicit sign-off) that is dated — introducing a mandatory human reviewer actor and a dated written decision as a process gate.
If the Phase 1 decision is 'erroneous,' a corrected brief must be attached or linked to the decision artifact — introducing an artifact-linking requirement.
The intentional path requires notifying downstream implementation planners that no tasks are to be derived from the approach — introducing an explicit downstream-notification step.
The erroneous path requires halting the chain, confirming the corrected brief is present, and queuing plan regeneration — three discrete sequenced steps on the error branch.
Exactly one path (intentional or erroneous) may be active simultaneously — mutual exclusion constraint on the two branches.

On the erroneous path, the corrected brief is fed to a plan-writer stage and then to an approach-writer stage, producing a replacement approach document — a two-stage regeneration pipeline with distinct roles.
The original no-op plan and approach must be archived or clearly superseded — an archival/provenance requirement for replaced artifacts.
Phase 3 is skipped entirely on the intentional path — an explicit skip condition tied to path selection.
Chain status must be set to either 'no-op confirmed' or 'rerun completed' and must not be left ambiguous — a constrained enumeration of allowed terminal states.
The decision trail from Phase 1 must be referenced in the chain metadata — a cross-phase referential integrity requirement.
Downstream implementation planners must be able to determine whether any tasks follow from the chain without reading the full chain — a discoverability/summary-access constraint on chain metadata.

A reviewer examines the technical approach document to determine whether the no-op signal was intentional or erroneous, and records a dated decision artifact (comment, ticket update, or explicit sign-off).

The chain branches on the Phase 1 decision: the intentional path notifies downstream implementation planners that no tasks are to be derived; the erroneous path halts the chain, confirms a corrected brief is present, and queues plan regeneration. Exactly one path is taken.

On the erroneous path, the corrected brief is fed to a plan-writer stage and then to an approach-writer stage, producing a replacement approach document; the original no-op plan and approach are archived or clearly marked as superseded.

Chain metadata (status, timestamp, decision trail) is updated at close-out; chain status is set to either 'no-op confirmed' or 'rerun completed' — never left ambiguous — so downstream implementation planners can determine without reading the full chain whether any tasks follow from the run.

A valid feature request brief must be supplied as input before the plan can be written — this is a new prerequisite constraint a developer or process owner would implement as an input validation gate.
The brief must describe three specific elements: the problem being solved, the intended users, and the desired outcome — these are enumerated content requirements for the input document.
The output business plan must cover both a summary section and an acceptance criteria section — these are specified deliverable components of the plan.
A valid feature request brief is required as input before a business plan can be produced.
The feature request brief must describe the problem being solved, the intended users, and the desired outcome.
A complete business plan produced from the brief will cover both a summary section and acceptance criteria section.
A valid feature request brief must be supplied as input before any plan can be produced — this is a prerequisite gate not implied by any of the (undefined) ground-truth claims.
The brief must describe the problem being solved, the intended users, and the desired outcome — three specific required fields for a conformant input.
A complete business plan must cover at minimum a summary section and acceptance criteria section — two specific required output sections.

The artifact states a blocking prerequisite: work cannot proceed until a valid business plan is provided that describes the problem being solved, the intended users, and the desired outcome — establishing an explicit gate condition not traceable to any ground-truth claim.
A stakeholder responsible for supplying the feature request brief must be identified and contacted before work can proceed.
The minimum required inputs for a valid brief are: problem statement, intended users, and desired outcome — all three must be present.
A valid business plan derived from the brief must be reviewed and accepted by the engineering lead before downstream phases are unblocked.
No architecture, domain model, workflows, or contracts can be specified until a valid business plan is provided.
A stakeholder must be identified and contacted to supply a feature request brief before any further work can proceed.
The minimum required inputs for the brief are specified as exactly three elements: problem statement, intended users, and desired outcome.
Receipt of a valid business plan must be formally documented.
The feature request brief must be accessible to the engineering team (not just received privately).
The brief must contain at least one identified user type (not zero, not a specific number greater than one).
A valid business plan derived from the brief must be reviewed and accepted by the engineering lead specifically (a named role) before downstream phases can proceed.
No architecture, domain model, workflows, or contracts can be specified until the valid business plan is provided — these four deliverable types are explicitly gated.
The feature request brief must contain a problem statement, at least one identified user type, and a stated desired outcome before any further phases can proceed.
A valid business plan derived from the brief must be reviewed and accepted by the engineering lead before architecture, domain model, workflows, and contracts can be specified.
A valid brief must contain at minimum: a problem statement, a proposed solution or capability, and intended users or stakeholders before the plan-writer prompt is invoked.
Once a valid brief is provided, this document is replaced with a complete business plan covering the summary and acceptance criteria derived from that brief.
A valid feature request brief must be supplied as input before the plan-writer prompt is invoked.
The brief must contain at minimum: a problem statement, a proposed solution or capability, and intended users or stakeholders.
Once a valid brief is provided, this document is replaced with a complete business plan covering the summary and acceptance criteria derived from that brief.
A valid feature request brief must be supplied as input before the plan-writer prompt is invoked — the artifact treats absence of a brief as a blocking condition.
The brief must contain at minimum three elements: a problem statement, a proposed solution or capability, and intended users or stakeholders.
Once a valid brief is provided, this document is replaced with a complete business plan covering summary and acceptance criteria derived from that brief.
Feature request brief must contain at minimum a problem statement, a proposed capability, and identified users or stakeholders before any downstream steps proceed.
Business plan produced from the brief must be non-null and must reference the brief's content.
Technical approach must contain a named architecture, at least one domain entity, at least one workflow, and at least one contract or API surface.
Domain entities, value objects, and aggregates are to be defined and implemented as importable units.
Repository interfaces must define at least create, read, update, and delete operations where applicable.
Unit tests must cover all entity invariants and repository contract tests must pass against at least one storage adapter.
Each workflow named in the approach document must have a corresponding implemented handler or service class.
Integration tests must cover at least the happy path and one error path per workflow.
Every contract defined in the approach document must have a corresponding implemented endpoint or interface.
Contract tests or schema validation tests must exist and pass for each API surface.
No workflow may be reachable through an undocumented surface.
At least one end-to-end test per identified user type must pass in a production-equivalent environment.
Developer documentation must accurately reflect the implemented contracts and workflows before delivery.
A feature request brief must contain at minimum a problem statement, a proposed capability, and identified users or stakeholders before any downstream planning can proceed.
The business plan produced from the brief must be non-null and must reference the brief's content to be considered valid.
The technical approach must contain a named architecture, at least one domain entity, at least one workflow, and at least one contract or API surface to be considered valid.
No workflow may be reachable through an undocumented surface — every reachable endpoint or interface must correspond to a defined contract.
End-to-end tests must cover at least one complete user journey per identified user type and must pass in a production-equivalent environment before the feature is considered delivery-ready.
The artifact states that a valid feature request brief must be supplied as input before any plan can be written.
The artifact states the brief must describe a problem or opportunity, the desired outcome, and any known constraints.
The artifact commits to rewriting the plan with a proper Summary and Acceptance Criteria once a valid brief is provided.
Architecture cannot be specified until the business plan summary and acceptance criteria are populated — a blocking prerequisite condition introduced by the artifact.
Domain model, entities, and invariants cannot be derived until the business plan is populated — a blocking prerequisite condition introduced by the artifact.
No workflows can be derived from the current plan — a blocking prerequisite condition introduced by the artifact.
No contracts can be derived from the current plan — a blocking prerequisite condition introduced by the artifact.
The business plan's summary section must contain at least one non-empty paragraph describing the feature's purpose.
The business plan's acceptance criteria section must contain at least three testable, unambiguous conditions.
No section of the plan may reference a dependency on another empty section.
A reviewer must be able to read the plan alone and derive the intended feature without additional context.
The business plan's summary section must contain at least one non-empty paragraph describing the feature's purpose.
The business plan's acceptance criteria section must contain at least three testable, unambiguous conditions.
No section of the plan may reference a dependency on another empty section.
A reviewer must be able to read the plan alone and derive the intended feature without additional context (self-contained plan requirement).
The plan's summary section must contain at least one non-empty paragraph describing the feature's purpose.
The plan's acceptance criteria section must contain at least three testable, unambiguous conditions.
No section of the plan may reference a dependency on another empty section.
A reviewer must be able to read the plan alone and derive the intended feature without additional context.
The scope includes reviewing the existing business plan document and identifying all empty or placeholder sections.
The scope includes populating the summary section with a clear statement of the feature's purpose and intended outcome.
The scope includes confirming that the populated plan is internally consistent with no contradictions between summary, domain, and criteria.
A valid feature request brief must be supplied as input before the plan can proceed.
The brief must contain at minimum: a problem statement, the intended users or beneficiaries, and a description of the desired outcome.
Upon receipt of a complete brief, a revised plan with meaningful Summary and Acceptance Criteria sections is produced and handed off to the technical lead.
A valid feature request brief must be supplied as input before the plan can be defined.
The brief must contain at minimum a problem statement, the intended users or beneficiaries, and a description of the desired outcome.
Upon receipt of a complete brief, a revised plan with meaningful Summary and Acceptance Criteria sections will be produced and handed off to the technical lead.
A complete brief must contain at minimum: a problem statement, intended users or beneficiaries, and a description of the desired outcome.
Upon receipt of a complete brief, a revised plan will be produced and handed off to the technical lead.
Brief Ingestion Service accepts a feature request brief as structured input, parses it, and stores a normalized representation with a status field (pending/invalid/valid).
Validation Engine checks each required field (problem_statement, intended_users, desired_outcome) for presence and non-empty content, returning field-level violation messages on failure.

Plan Generator is triggered only after a brief passes validation and produces a BusinessPlan with a Summary derived from problem statement and desired outcome, and Acceptance Criteria derived from desired outcome and intended users as testable conditions.
BusinessPlan versioning: regeneration increments the version field rather than creating a duplicate record (idempotent for a given brief identifier).
Handoff Notifier delivers the completed BusinessPlan to a configured downstream recipient (technical lead or planning queue) and creates a HandoffRecord capturing recipient and delivery timestamp.
Recipient descriptor is resolved by the Handoff Notifier against a configured registry of downstream consumers; transport details are not exposed to callers.
Status Query Interface accepts a brief identifier and returns the current status (pending/invalid/valid) plus a reference to the associated BusinessPlan if generated, intended for polling callers.
FeatureRequestBrief stores raw_input (the original submitted text) for auditability.
Resubmission workflow: a caller corrects a previously invalid brief and resubmits, triggering a new or updated FeatureRequestBrief record and restarting the full validation workflow.
Validation Interface is specified as stateless and side-effect-free; all persistence is handled by the caller.
Brief Ingestion Service accepts a feature request brief as structured input, parses it, and stores a normalized representation.
Validation Engine applies completeness rules checking each required field for presence and non-trivially populated content; rejects with field-level violations or approves.

Plan Generator is triggered only after a brief passes validation and produces a BusinessPlan with a Summary (derived from problem statement and desired outcome) and Acceptance Criteria (derived from desired outcome and intended users, expressed as testable conditions).
Handoff Notifier delivers the completed BusinessPlan to a configured downstream recipient (technical lead or planning queue) and creates a HandoffRecord capturing recipient and delivery timestamp.
FeatureRequestBrief domain model with fields: id, problem_statement, intended_users, desired_outcome, raw_input (preserved for auditability), submitted_at, and status (one of pending/invalid/valid).
ValidationResult domain model with fields: brief_id, passed (boolean), and violations (list of ValidationViolation records).
ValidationViolation domain model with fields: field and reason (human-readable explanation).
BusinessPlan domain model with fields: id, brief_id, summary, acceptance_criteria, generated_at, and version (incremented on regeneration rather than creating a duplicate).
HandoffRecord domain model with fields: plan_id, recipient, and delivered_at; one HandoffRecord is created per delivery.

Brief Submission Interface accepts problem statement (string, required), intended users (string or list, required), desired outcome (string, required), and optional metadata; returns brief identifier plus status on success or field-level violation messages on failure.
Validation Interface is stateless and side-effect-free; all persistence is handled by the caller, not the validation engine itself.
Plan Generation Interface is idempotent for a given brief identifier — regeneration increments the version field rather than creating a duplicate BusinessPlan record.
Handoff Interface resolves the recipient descriptor against a configured registry of downstream consumers and does not expose transport details to callers.

Status Query Interface accepts a brief identifier and returns current status (pending/invalid/valid) and, if a plan has been generated, a reference to the associated BusinessPlan; intended for polling callers.
Resubmission workflow: a caller who corrects a previously invalid brief triggers a new or updated FeatureRequestBrief record and the full validation workflow restarts from ingestion.
FeatureRequestBrief has a status field with three enumerated values: pending, invalid, valid — a brief moves through this lifecycle as it is ingested and validated.
The Validation Engine is stateless and side-effect-free; all persistence is handled by the caller (Validation Interface contract).
Plan Generation is idempotent for a given brief identifier — regeneration increments the BusinessPlan version field rather than creating a duplicate record.
The BusinessPlan includes a version field that is incremented on regeneration.
The Handoff Notifier resolves the recipient descriptor against a configured registry of downstream consumers; transport details are not exposed to callers.
A HandoffRecord is created per delivery, capturing recipient identity and a delivered_at timestamp.
A Status Query Interface allows callers to poll progress: given a brief identifier it returns current status and, if a plan has been generated, a reference to the associated BusinessPlan.
On resubmission after rejection, a new FeatureRequestBrief record is created (or the existing one updated, implementation-policy dependent) and the full validation workflow restarts.
The Brief Submission Interface returns either a success response with brief identifier and status, or an error response with a list of field-level violation messages.
The raw_input field on FeatureRequestBrief preserves the original submitted text for auditability.
The submitted_at timestamp is captured on FeatureRequestBrief at submission time.
The BusinessPlan's Acceptance Criteria are expressed as testable conditions derived from the desired outcome and intended users.

FeatureRequestBrief domain model with fields id, problem_statement, intended_users, desired_outcome, raw_input, submitted_at, and status; raw_input preserves original submitted text exactly as received
Brief Ingestion Service accepts a brief payload, normalizes it, and persists it with status pending
Brief Submission Interface parses incoming payload and returns a success response containing brief identifier and status
ValidationResult and ValidationViolation domain models

Validation Engine checks problem_statement, intended_users, and desired_outcome for presence and non-empty content; briefs failing validation are marked invalid; briefs passing are marked valid; field-level violation messages are returned to the caller on failure
Validation Interface is stateless: calling it twice with the same input produces the same result without side effects
BusinessPlan domain model with fields id, brief_id, summary, acceptance_criteria, generated_at, and version
Plan Generator derives summary from problem_statement and desired_outcome; derives acceptance_criteria from desired_outcome and intended_users as testable conditions
Idempotency constraint: regenerating a plan for the same brief increments version by 1 rather than creating a new unrelated record
An invalid brief does not trigger plan generation
HandoffRecord domain model with fields plan_id, recipient, and delivered_at
Handoff Notifier resolves recipient descriptors against a configured registry of downstream consumers; an unrecognized recipient causes a clear error and no HandoffRecord is created
Handoff Interface does not expose transport details (e.g. email address, queue URL) to callers
Caller receives a final notification confirming acceptance, plan generation, and handoff in a single response
Status Query Interface accepts a brief id and returns current status (pending, invalid, or valid) and a reference to the associated BusinessPlan if one exists

Resubmission handling: a corrected brief restarts the validation workflow from the start; the implementation policy (new record vs. update) is documented and enforced consistently so no brief ends in an ambiguous state
End-to-end integration test covering both the happy path (submit to validate to generate to handoff) and the rejection/resubmission path

A brief submitted with all required fields is persisted with status 'pending' and a generated id
The submission endpoint returns the brief id and status on success
The raw_input field preserves the original submitted text exactly as received
ValidationResult and ValidationViolation domain models are defined
The Validation Engine checks problem_statement, intended_users, and desired_outcome for presence and non-empty content; whitespace-only values are treated as empty
Briefs failing validation are marked 'invalid'; briefs passing are marked 'valid'
Field-level violation messages are returned to the caller on validation failure
A ValidationResult record is created for every brief, with violations empty when passing
Validation is stateless: calling it twice with the same input produces the same result without side effects
BusinessPlan domain model with fields id, brief_id, summary, acceptance_criteria, generated_at, and version
Plan summary is derived from problem_statement and desired_outcome; acceptance_criteria are derived from desired_outcome and intended_users as testable conditions
Regenerating a plan for the same brief_id increments version by 1 rather than creating a new unrelated record (idempotency)
An invalid brief does not trigger plan generation
HandoffRecord domain model with fields plan_id, recipient, and delivered_at
The Handoff Notifier resolves recipient descriptors against a configured registry of downstream consumers; an unrecognized recipient causes a clear error and no HandoffRecord is created
The Handoff Interface does not expose transport details (e.g., email address, queue URL) to callers
The caller receives a final notification confirming acceptance, plan generation, and handoff in a single response
Status Query Interface: querying by brief id returns current status (pending, invalid, or valid) and a reference to the associated BusinessPlan if one exists
A resubmitted brief after rejection undergoes full validation from the start; if it passes, a new BusinessPlan is generated and a HandoffRecord is created
Resubmission policy (new record or update) is applied consistently — no brief ends in an ambiguous state
An end-to-end integration test covers the complete happy-path flow (submit → validate → generate → handoff) and the rejection/resubmission path without manual intervention

Brief Submission Interface returns brief id and status on success
raw_input field preserves the original submitted text exactly as received
Briefs can be retrieved by id via the Status Query Interface showing pending status
